findings.description: _client_key trusts the client-supplied X-Forwarded-For header and uses its leftmost value as identity, so an attacker can spoof a fresh value per request and fully bypass rate limiting (CWE-290 auth bypass by spoofing; the leftmost XFF entry is always client-controlled, not the proxy-observed peer).

self._hits is a defaultdict(list) keyed by the spoofable client key, and check() materializes a bucket per distinct key with no global cap, enabling unbounded-memory DoS (CWE-770) since sweep() is never invoked automatically.

Off-by-one: 'len(hits) <= self.limit' admits and appends when count equals the limit, so exactly limit+1 requests pass per window — the configured limit is under-enforced by one (CWE-697 incorrect comparison).

current_usage() reads self._hits[key] on a defaultdict, inserting an empty bucket for unseen keys; this read-style method mutates state and amplifies the unbounded-key memory DoS when reachable with attacker-controlled keys (CWE-770).
The attacker-controlled key (from X-Forwarded-For) is logged verbatim, allowing CRLF/control-character log injection and log forging (CWE-117).

hits.pop(0) on a list is O(n) per pruned element (and sweep/current_usage do full list scans); under attacker-driven bursts this algorithmic cost is a secondary DoS vector — use collections.deque with popleft() (CWE-407).
